label: verify-39-cal-4th-205
system:
You are the citation verifier in a legal-exploration pipeline. You are given one authority as cited by the pipeline's components, plus snippets showing how it was used. Using web search (prefer primary/official sources: leginfo.legislature.ca.gov, courts.ca.gov, law.justia.com, casetext.com, codes.findlaw.com, fppc.ca.gov), determine:
1. Does the authority exist?
2. Is the citation form correct (reporter cite, year, section number)?
3. Does it plausibly stand for what the snippets use it for?

Statuses: VERIFIED (exists, cite essentially right, usage consistent with what you found); MISCHARACTERIZED (exists but does not support the claimed use, or the cite attaches to the wrong case/section); NOT_FOUND (cannot locate after genuine searching); CONTESTED (real sources point in conflicting directions, or scope is genuinely unsettled); UNCLEAR (could not determine within a reasonable search).

You are NOT determining whether the authority is still good law (no Shepardizing) — say what you actually confirmed, no more. Be strict: a close-but-wrong reporter cite or a holding stretched past what sources show is MISCHARACTERIZED, not VERIFIED.
prompt:
## Authority as cited (variants seen in the documents)

- 39 Cal.4th 205

## How the documents used it

[delphi] … including in-rationale reference)
- *Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano* (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference)
- *Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil* (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference; one panelist spelled "Vejil")
- Cal. Const. art. XIII C / §1(e) (Prop 26) — 6
- Cal. Const. art. X, §2 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference)
- Water Code §375 — 6
- *Coziahr v…

[tetlock] …classified as a tax requiring Proposition 26/218 voter approval, or fall under some other exemption (e.g., tied to Water Code §375 conservation-rate authority)? (P=85)
- Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 held that volumetric, metered charges for ongoing retail water delivery to a parcel are 'fees for a property related service' under Art. XIII D §6(b)(4) — this is the closest controlling precedent and cuts strongly agai…

[dialectic] …fense and, if defeated early, forces the District onto the merits where it is most exposed.

- **Retail water delivery charges are the paradigm property-related fee.** *Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil* (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 held that charges for ongoing water delivery service, imposed as an incident of property ownership, are property-related fees subject to Article XIII D, § 6 — notwithstanding that they are usage/metered-based rather tha…

## Context

The underlying question is a California public-agency hypothetical: q2 — Drought surcharge funding low-income rebates (Prop 218).

Verify this authority per your instructions.

canonical_cite.description: the correct full citation, if determinable

note.description: 1-3 sentences: what was confirmed or refuted, and the correct cite

text: {"status":"VERIFIED","canonical_cite":"Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205","note":"The case exists and the citation form (39 Cal.4th 205, decided July 24, 2006) is correct. Its core holding — that charges for ongoing metered/volumetric domestic water delivery to a parcel are \"fees\" or \"charges\" imposed as an incident of property ownership for a \"property related service\" subject to Article XIII D (and that Art. XIII C §3 initiative power lets voters reduce, but not impose preapproval on, such rates) — is accurately reflected in the [delphi] and [dialectic] uses (which cite it generally to Art. XIII D §6). The [tetlock] snippet's specific pincite to \"§6(b)(4)\" is imprecise: Bighorn's classification holding is not tied to that subdivision (which concerns actual-use/immediate-availability), and secondary sources confirm the opinion does not analyze 6(b)(3)/(b)(4) in detail — that proportionality/availability analysis is more associated with later cases like Capistrano. This is a minor subsection-level imprecision in one usage, not a misattribution of the case or its central, well-established holding.","source_urls":["https://www.courtlistener.com/opinion/2631763/bighorn-desert-view-water-agency-v-verjil/","https://scocal.stanford.edu/opinion/bighorn-desert-etc-v-verjil-33651","https://www.waterlawjournal.com/upgrading-conservation-pricing-proposition-218-smart-meters-and-the-step-beyond-tiered-rates/"]}

data.canonical_cite: Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205
data.note: The case exists and the citation form (39 Cal.4th 205, decided July 24, 2006) is correct. Its core holding — that charges for ongoing metered/volumetric domestic water delivery to a parcel are "fees" or "charges" imposed as an incident of property ownership for a "property related service" subject to Article XIII D (and that Art. XIII C §3 initiative power lets voters reduce, but not impose preapproval on, such rates) — is accurately reflected in the [delphi] and [dialectic] uses (which cite it generally to Art. XIII D §6). The [tetlock] snippet's specific pincite to "§6(b)(4)" is imprecise: Bighorn's classification holding is not tied to that subdivision (which concerns actual-use/immediate-availability), and secondary sources confirm the opinion does not analyze 6(b)(3)/(b)(4) in detail — that proportionality/availability analysis is more associated with later cases like Capistrano. This is a minor subsection-level imprecision in one usage, not a misattribution of the case or its central, well-established holding.
